CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Oct-07-2020 Continued To Dec-16-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-14-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. CHRISTINA KIM, Esq. of Gilsleider, McMahon, Molinelli & Phan (Tel: 925-446-3115) for defendant Nam Chung;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT NAM CHUNG'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

The Court notes that defense counsel did not properly contest the court's tentative ruling. As counsel for the opposing side is not present, the Court precludes argument by defense counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Nam Hyuk Chung's demurrer is overruled.

The demurrer to the third cause of action is overruled. The negligence per se doctrine creates a presumption of negligence that affects the burden of proof. (Evid. Code, section 669 subd. (a)(1)-(3).) The court recognizes that negligence per se is not a cause of action; however, plaintiff may plead different theories of liability. (See Mendoza v. Continental Sales Co. (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1395, 1402.) This cause of action is adequately pled. (See Compl. NP1-5.)

The demurrer to the fourth cause of action is overruled. A claim for IIED requires outrageous conduct on behalf of the defendant. Whether defendant's alleged conduct in driving while intoxicated, running a red light, and thereby causing personal injury, constitutes outrageous conduct presents a question for the trier of fact that cannot be determined on demurrer. (See Plotnik v. Meihaus (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 1590, 1614 ["Where reasonable men can differ, the jury determines whether the conduct has been extreme and outrageous to result in liability."].)

Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare and submit a proposed order.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/EPS)

Defendants Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Complaint
Off Calendar Sep-14-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. CHRISTINA KIM, Esq. of Gilsleider, McMahon, Molinelli & Phan (Tel: 925-446-3115) for defendant Nam Chung;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT NAM CHUNG'S Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiff's Complaint.

The Court notes that defense counsel did not properly contest the court's tentative ruling. As counsel for the opposing side is not present, the Court precludes argument by defense counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Nam Hyuk Chung's motion to strike is denied. Plaintiff's allegation that defendant was intoxicated is directly relevant to plaintiff's negligence and other claims, and is not improper. Operating a motor vehicle while intoxicated may be the basis for an award of punitive damages, particularly when combined with other conduct (such as running a red light) that may evince a conscious disregard of safety and the probable injury of others. (Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, 897 ["One who willfully consumes alcoholic beverages to the point of intoxication, knowing that he thereafter must operate a motor vehicle, thereby combining sharply impaired physical and mental faculties with a vehicle capable of great force and speed, reasonably may be held to exhibit a conscious disregard of the safety of others."]; Dawes v. Superior Court (1980) 111 Cal.App.3d 82, 88-89; see also Peterson v. Superior Court (1982) 31 Cal.3d 147, 151 ["If allowed, such allegations could support a finding of malice within the meaning of Civil Code section 3294 as interpreted by Taylor, and thus an award of punitive damages"].) Defendant's attempts to limit Taylor and Dawes to their facts are unpersuasive.

Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare and submit a proposed order.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/EPS)

Defendants Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay
Off Calendar Sep-14-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. CHRISTINA KIM, Esq. of Gilsleider, McMahon, Molinelli & Phan (Tel: 925-446-3115) for defendant Nam Chung;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT NAM CHUNG'S Motion to Stay.

The Court notes that defense counsel did not properly contest the court's tentative ruling. As counsel for the opposing side is not present, the Court precludes argument by defense counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion to stay discovery is granted in part. The court stays defendant's deposition and any written discovery directed to defendant so as to preserve his Fifth Amendment privilege against self-incrimination in the parallel criminal proceedings. (Pacers, Inc. v. Superior Court (1984) 162 Cal.App.3d 686, 690 ["[W]hen both civil and criminal proceedings arise out of the same or related transactions, an objecting party is generally entitled to a stay of discovery in the civil action until disposition of the criminal matter."].) The stay does not extend to other matters, including "medical discovery" (e.g., discovery relating to plaintiff's alleged injuries and damages), which represents an appropriate balance of the parties' interests. (See id. ["Where, as here, a defendant's silence is constitutionally guaranteed, the court should weigh the parties' competing interests with a view toward accommodating the interests of both parties, if possible."]; Keating v. Office of Thrift Supervision (9th Cir. 1995) 45 F.3d 322, 325.)

Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare and submit a proposed order.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/EPS)
